title: Rootless podman run: mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/530a853f5...: permission denied: OCI permission denied
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

Can't run container with cgroup managed options --cpus= --memory=

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. build image

2. run container wth `--cpus=` `--memory=` options

3.

**Describe the results you received:**

```
$ podman run -it --cpus=10 --memory=24g my_img
Error: container_linux.go:380: starting container process caused: process_linux.go:385: applying cgroup configuration for process caused: rootless needs no limits + no cgrouppath when no permission is granted for cgroups: mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/530a853f5d02c0a95c7764643a6b15d391b8fe405704f61adc72548d702ee96c: permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

Getting container's command line

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.3.1
API Version:  3.3.1
Go Version:   go1.17.1
Built:        Sat Sep 11 20:06:22 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
I think this is expected. Your rootless user has no permission for the cgroupfs. On systemd distros we use systemd to gain the extra privileges to create cgroups.
@giuseppe Can you confirm?

> I think this is expected. Your rootless user has no permission for the cgroupfs. On systemd distros we use systemd to gain the extra privileges to create cgroups.

But how it works on systemd?
I have the next sys fs structure:
```
# ls -la /sys/fs/cgroup
total 0
dr-xr-xr-x 12 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 .
drwxr-xr-x  9 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 ..
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 1
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 bluetooth
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 c1
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cgroup.controllers
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cgroup.max.depth
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cgroup.max.descendants
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 cgroup.procs
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cgroup.stat
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cgroup.subtree_control
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cgroup.threads
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cpu.pressure
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cpuset.cpus.effective
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cpuset.mems.effective
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 cpu.stat
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 dbus
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 display-manager
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 elogind
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 io.cost.model
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 io.cost.qos
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 io.pressure
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 io.stat
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 lvmetad
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 memory.numa_stat
-rw-r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 memory.pressure
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 memory.stat
-r--r--r--  1 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 misc.capacity
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 NetworkManager
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:01 ntpd
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root 0 Sep 14 10:00 udev
```

> I think this is expected. Your rootless user has no permission for the cgroupfs. On systemd distros we use systemd to gain the extra privileges to create cgroups.
> @giuseppe Can you confirm?

yes, or you need to create the cgroup before.



> But how it works on systemd?

systemd will create a new cgroup for the user.

So there are two alternatives:

- switch to the systemd backend.
- create the cgroup with root and chown it and delegate it to the user you are using for creating the container.  Then use `--cgroup-parent` to force using that cgroup
